meta.description: Phase 1 hand-built Kant cluster + Phase 3 backbone (bb-v1) over the full Ethics registry

nodes.label: Categorical Imperative
nodes.definition: Kant's supreme principle of morality: act only according to that maxim whereby you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law. Unconditional, applying to all rational agents regardless of their desires.

nodes.label: Hypothetical Imperative
nodes.definition: A command of reason that applies only conditionally, given some desired end: 'if you want X, do Y'. Contrasted by Kant with the unconditional categorical imperative.

nodes.label: Autonomy
nodes.definition: Self-legislation of the rational will: the capacity to act according to laws one gives oneself, rather than from external causes or inclination. For Kant, the ground of human dignity.

nodes.label: Deontology
nodes.definition: A normative ethical theory that judges the morality of actions by their conformity to duties or rules, rather than by their consequences.

nodes.label: Consequentialism
nodes.definition: The family of normative ethical theories holding that the moral rightness of an act depends only on the value of its consequences.

nodes.label: Utilitarianism
nodes.definition: The consequentialist theory that the right action is the one that maximises overall well-being or utility, classically 'the greatest happiness of the greatest number'.

nodes.label: Virtue Ethics
nodes.definition: A normative approach centring on character and the virtues — stable excellences of the person — rather than on rules or consequences. Rooted in Aristotle's eudaimonism.

nodes.label: Normative Ethics
nodes.definition: The branch of moral philosophy that studies what makes actions right or wrong and what we ought to do, comprising principally deontology, consequentialism, and virtue ethics.

nodes.label: Immanuel Kant
nodes.definition: German philosopher (1724–1804), central figure of modern philosophy. Author of the three Critiques; founder of deontological ethics grounded in the categorical imperative and autonomy of the will.

nodes.label: David Hume
nodes.definition: Scottish empiricist philosopher (1711–1776). His scepticism about causation and reason famously woke Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber'.

nodes.label: Jeremy Bentham
nodes.definition: English philosopher and reformer (1748–1832), founder of classical utilitarianism and the principle of utility.

nodes.label: John Stuart Mill
nodes.definition: English philosopher (1806–1873) who refined utilitarianism with qualitative distinctions among pleasures, and defended liberty and representative government.

nodes.label: Aristotle
nodes.definition: Greek philosopher (384–322 BC). His Nicomachean Ethics grounds virtue ethics in eudaimonia — flourishing achieved through excellent activity of the soul.

nodes.label: German Idealism
nodes.definition: The philosophical movement (c. 1781–1840) emerging from Kant's critical philosophy, developed by Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel.

nodes.label: Free Will
nodes.definition: ability of agents to be the ultimate source or originator of their choices, free from external determination

nodes.label: Determinism
nodes.definition: philosophical belief that all events are determined completely by previously existing causes

nodes.label: Compatibilism
nodes.definition: belief that free will and determinism are mutually compatible to one another

nodes.label: Moral Responsibility
nodes.definition: status of morally deserving praise, blame, reward, or punishment for an act or omission, in accordance with one's moral obligations

nodes.label: Moral Status
nodes.definition: description of intrinsic self-worth and dignity

nodes.label: Moral Dilemma
nodes.definition: decision-making problem between two conflicting moral imperatives, neither of which is unambiguously acceptable or preferable

nodes.label: Doctrine of Double Effect
nodes.definition: set of ethical criteria permitting certain actions when one's otherwise legitimate act may also cause harm

nodes.label: Thomas Aquinas
nodes.definition: Italian Dominican friar, philosopher, Catholic priest, and Doctor of the Church (1225–1274)

nodes.label: Natural Law
nodes.definition: system of law that is purportedly determined by nature, and is thus universal

nodes.label: Supererogation
nodes.definition: acts which are good but not morally required to be done

nodes.label: Duty
nodes.definition: legal or moral requirement to take a certain course of action

nodes.label: Practical Reason
nodes.definition: the use of reason to decide how to act

nodes.label: Eudaimonia
nodes.definition: Aristotelian term for happiness or welfare

nodes.label: Well-Being
nodes.definition: measure of how well life is to someone or a group with factors such as health, happiness and satisfaction

nodes.label: Justice
nodes.definition: broad idea of a situation where people receive that which they deserve

nodes.label: Virtue
nodes.definition: morally positive trait or quality deemed to be morally good

nodes.label: Rights
nodes.definition: fundamental legal, social, or ethical principles of freedom or entitlement according to some legal system, social convention, or ethical theory

nodes.label: Psychological Egoism
nodes.definition: The view that true altruism in humans is impossible

nodes.label: Altruism
nodes.definition: principle or practice of concern for the welfare of others

nodes.label: Akrasia
nodes.definition: lack of self-control or state of acting against one's better judgment

nodes.label: Metaethics
nodes.definition: branch of ethics seeking to understand ethical properties

nodes.label: Scholasticism
nodes.definition: method of critical thought which dominated teaching by the academics ("scholastics", or "schoolmen") of medieval universities in Europe from about 1100 to 1700

nodes.label: Hedonism
nodes.definition: philosophy of pleasure as the highest value

nodes.label: Prescriptivism
nodes.definition: metaethical view that ethical sentences function similarly to imperatives which are universalizable—whoever makes a moral judgment is committed to the same judgment in any situation where the same relevant facts obtain

nodes.label: Moral Cognitivism
nodes.definition: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences express propositions and can therefore be true or false

nodes.label: Non-Cognitivism
nodes.definition: the meta-ethical view that ethical sentences do not express propositions and thus cannot be true or false

nodes.label: Emotivism
nodes.definition: metaethical view that moral statements are expressions of emotional attitudes rather than objective truths

nodes.label: Expressivism
nodes.definition: metaethical theory that moral sentences are not descriptive, but express an evaluative attitude toward an object

nodes.label: Allan Gibbard
nodes.definition: American philosopher and social choice theorist

nodes.label: Moral Nihilism
nodes.definition: meta-ethical view that nothing is intrinsically moral or immoral

nodes.label: Moral Relativism
nodes.definition: philosophical positions about the differences in moral judgments across peoples and cultures

nodes.label: Moral Intuitionism
nodes.definition: family of views in moral epistemology

nodes.label: Naturalistic Fallacy
nodes.definition: ethical argument asserting that it is fallacious to explain something good reductively

nodes.label: Open Question Argument
nodes.definition: philosophical argument put forward by British philosopher G. E. Moore, to refute the equating of the property of goodness with some non-moral property, X, whether naturalistic (e.g. pleasure) or supernatural

nodes.label: Rationalism
nodes.definition: philosophical view that reason should be the chief source of knowledge

nodes.label: Is-Ought Problem
nodes.definition: philosophical problem articulated by David Hume in 1739 about how one can deduce prescriptive statements (what ought to be) from descriptive statements (what is)

nodes.label: Rule Utilitarianism
nodes.definition: form of utilitarianism that says an action is right as it conforms to a rule that leads to the greatest good

nodes.label: Kingdom of Ends
nodes.definition: Kant’s thought experiment about a world in which all humans are treated as ends, not as means

nodes.label: Practical Wisdom
nodes.definition: ancient Greek word for a type of wisdom or intelligence

nodes.label: Ethical Egoism
nodes.definition: ethical position that moral agents should act in their own self-interest

nodes.label: Divine Command Theory
nodes.definition: theory that morality is commanded by, and originates only from, the divine

nodes.label: Stoicism
nodes.definition: school of Hellenistic philosophy who held that the practice of virtue suffices to attain eudaimonia

nodes.label: Empiricism
nodes.definition: theory that states that knowledge comes only or primarily from sensory experience

nodes.label: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
nodes.definition: Translation of Kant's Groundwork published in 1997

nodes.label: The Metaphysics of Morals
nodes.definition: work of political and moral philosophy by Immanuel Kant

nodes.label: Leviathan
aliases: Leviathan or The Matter, Forme and Power of a Commonwealth Ecclesiasticall and Civil

nodes.label: An Enquiry Concerning the Principles of Morals
An Introduction to the Principles of Morals and Legislation
nodes.definition: intro to the Principles of Morals and Legislation

nodes.label: On Liberty
nodes.definition: non-fiction work by John Stuart Mill

nodes.label: Language, Truth and Logic
nodes.definition: philosophical book by A. J. Ayer

nodes.label: The Right and the Good
nodes.definition: 1930 book by the Scottish philosopher David Ross

nodes.label: A Theory of Justice
nodes.definition: work of political philosophy and ethics by John Rawls

nodes.label: What We Owe to Each Other

evidence.quote: Immanuel Kant (1724–1804) argued that the supreme principle of morality is a principle of rationality that he dubbed the “Categorical Imperative” (CI).
